speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six of the interview with Elizabeth Etinger on October the 11th, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I start to work in music hall or musical comedy in Liegnitz. By the time I started, I was well-known already as this. I had the record. When I was sick, they put my record, songs, just to entertain. People knew straight away it's Lisa singing. And that was, I think, up to December '46 and January '47--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, just a minute. I know that was-- it couldn't be '45. '45? Didn't you suggest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes, it was '46. It was in January or February '46 that I was working still in music hall there. And in the meantime, you see, we were like army workers, and every year, army workers get some sort of material for your pleasure to do whatever you want. And when I got this material, I decided to do a coat for winter, and I went to look for tailor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I found a tailor that-- to my friend, she knew the tailor. She said, look, go there. He's a good tailor, and he will make you a good coat. So I went down there, and I give him material. And I said what I wanted. And he said, OK. And he said in two days to come and have a-- Fitting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fitting. So I did-- in two days, I went for a fitting. When fitting finish, I-- by the way, this tailor was a Jewish boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Liegnitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Liegnitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Poland. And this guy, called [INAUDIBLE], now we knew each other because I was very grateful that he going to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to-- back to the theater because we had this day, whether it was or-- I don't remember exactly which play was then, but in my, my plays that-- costume and all sort of thing was in a room together with the few. When I come to the room to go in, I heard scream. And they was [INAUDIBLE], and I thought, uh-oh, something is not right. Because when I just walked in, they all quiet. Not a word.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only-- I have to say that I was only one Jewish in, in the whole theater. There was only-- director of the theater was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other was Russian. Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, everybody know. You see, in Russia, if you're a Jew, it's in passport, Jew. They can't say they Russian if you have passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: But under Chotowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, as soon-- as soon they free me, they knew that I Jew. As soon Russian free me, and when I went to work, they already knew that I am Jewish, that I am not a Chotowa. I am Beri. That my father is David Moishe. Everything what I am, I told them. So they knew that I am a Jew. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: So there is no more Chotowa. No more other names. My-- my everybody got the real name. But I was surprised. Why don't they talk while I'm here? So I've got a-- there's [INAUDIBLE], one, which work with me, I ask her, what's happening? So she told me that we had a [INAUDIBLE]. It's heading role in the musical comedy, which was brought from Moscow in the theater. So apparently, the director sack her and said she can go home. And because he was a Jew, so what happened? They blame a Jew. The Russians gone. He sacked her. She wasn't a bad person. What was the reason that he sack her? I didn't know. It wasn't my-- but I just said that, you see, there is bad Jews and good Jews. Because of bad Jews, good Jews suffering, too. Because I start-- I knew that they don't really want to talk because I am a Jew. They stop talking, so that's what I said. And somebody went to told-- and told Secret Service. What did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they said that I said-- in Russia I will tell you, they will now-- yes, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] But you see, I could say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like there is Jews and the bloody Jews. Because of bloody Jews, Jews are bad, too. It's like in every one nationality. It's nothing to it. But you see, this time, you should-- you shouldn't say [? shit ?] because that was a very bad say. And as I said before, Stalin said-- Stalin could put in prison for the word [? shit, ?] but there was difference between me saying shit and Russian or Ukraine or others saying [? shit. ?] I could say [? shit ?] because I am [? shit, ?] and I can criticize that there is bad Jews and good Jews. And because of bad Jews, good Jews suffer, too. But the person went and told what I said, and that was enough. In two days, they called me in the Secret Service, special Secret Service, and told me. And I remember it was-- it was in January, not February, because it was very cold, very bad-- it was winter. I didn't found then yet not my parents, not my sister, nobody. I was looking all the time. As soon they just free me, I was looking. I couldn't find nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this, this was Captain Smirnov, I remember. He called me and said that we have special transport for people to Russia, and you can go to Russia now. We will provide with special wagon and things like this. And I look at them, and said, why now? I said-- [BACKROUND NOISE] I didn't found nobody yet, and it's such a winter, such a cold. Why couldn't we wait for summer? I know they needed me here. Nobody told me that they don't need me. Why all of a sudden? And he couldn't answer. So I told him, Captain Smirnov, if you think-- it's captain-- if you think I don't know why, you're mistaken. It's because I said this word [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. I can say it. I am a Jew. You know it. I can criticize. You better look, watch others, who says it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he white like snow white, and I knew I had it right. And he didn't ask for nothing. He just kept quiet, but he said, doesn't matter. Tomorrow at 10 o'clock, you have to take your luggage and have, have to be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what can I say? I say nothing. But I was so upset. I went to Colonel Zhitkovskii. He was the director of the [INAUDIBLE] of Red Army. And I ask him, do you want me to go to Russia? You don't need me here? He said, what! We do need you. Who said so? So I said, Captain Smirnov said that I have to go to Russia. He [INAUDIBLE] the and-- but Special Service captain is more than general. General can't say nothing if captain from Special Service say yes. So the-- this colonel said, oh, I'm so sorry I have to lose you. I have to lose you. I can't do nothing about it. So I knew. I knew I was right. So what I did, I start away from there, went to this tailor, and said, you have to help me because I don't want to go to Russia now. If I want, I go on my terms, when I want it. When I find my family, then I go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, OK. He rang. They was Habonim. My, my husband was in Gordonia. That's a youth of Habonim. He rang Habonim, Gordonia, this organization. They have a kibbutz in Liegnitz, and they organize. At 8 o'clock at night the same day, I had to take only taxi. My husband was waiting there. I took the-- he send the taxi with taxi [? cab ?]. I put my few things, and we went for the taxi straight to Habonim, this Gordonia, because they was more younger. In the older, then we went to Habonim in [INAUDIBLE] kibbutz, and there start again. I start again. I have to save myself. I have to change my name. Yeah, we stay in this-- me and my husband stay in this Gordonia Liegnitz for three nights. On the fourth night, they organize with the Habonim kibbutz in [INAUDIBLE]. And they sent a truck, little truck, gas. They sent there. At night, I put shawl, a big shawl, on my-- because on the way was [INAUDIBLE]. Everywhere was Russian [INAUDIBLE]. They stopped any transport that goes, and they don't know. They stop and want to know who is in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a very big risk, but we were just hoping that nothing will happen. I was sitting right in the corner there. They put something black over me, and I couldn't see. My husband could-- he was Polish, so he could go in front with them. I was alone in the back. And they took us from this kibbutz to the [INAUDIBLE] kibbutz by the tram. Can I just clarify the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] or Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: From, from-- Vrotslav. Sorry. From Liegnitz that took us to Vrotslav. That's where I come here from, Vrotslav. In Vrotslav, in Habonim-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kibbutz. In [INAUDIBLE], I remember, it was a, a woman's [INAUDIBLE] we got married, me and my husband, and they made the whole party of marriage. But to be sure everything under control, because they found out-- after we left Liegnitz kibbutz, they come people asking for me from army, and they say they don't know. So they have to organize for me new papers. I couldn't be anymore Beri. I couldn't be anymore as I-- as I really was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they organize repatriation papers. The people who were sent already to Israel. The list-- paper they left, and there was one paper who fits me. Sala Ganz from Horodenka, born in 10/22-- 10 of 6, '22. So they give me this paper. I was married on with these papers. So from now on, I'm not anymore Beri. I am Sala Ganz from Horodenka, living in Vrotslav, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to run. After all what I did, what I come through, working against Germans, [INAUDIBLE] of Russian. I have to run from Russian as a deserter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to save myself again, to be able to find my parents and my sisters, I have to change my name again. That's my story. So I am-- and I am Sala Ganz, maiden name. Married, my husband Gershon Etinger, so my maiden is Ganz. My name is Etinger, Elizabeth. Right? Can you tell us just a little bit about the kibbutz and its purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, kibbutz. The purpose of kibbutz was to prepare people to go to Israel. And most people from kibbutz went. That was illegal. That was [INAUDIBLE] Aliyah, Aliyah to Israel. It was illegal. It was done by, by ship, and they have to go to Czechoslovakia, some of them to Austria, to [INAUDIBLE], and it was all illegal. So they have to have paper for them, all sort of things to prepare them to live in Israel. Most of them moved from kibbutz to Israel. There was a few families which-- which knew about Israel, were prepared. So they went to first Aliyah. A lot of them didn't-- was shot on the way to Israel. But now-- most probably now there's a lot of people which want to go straight away to Israel, went to Israel, and were shot. They were caught on the border. I know my friend, two friends, never went-- never see the Israel because on the way to Israel, on the border, they were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were preparing, too, to go to Israel. In what way do they help you prepare apart from the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all-- look, we have Pesach. We have everything. We knew what is all up to now by Jewish about how to celebrate, how-- a bris. How [INAUDIBLE]. All sort of things, which most of them didn't know. Polish Jew, yes, but not Russian Jew. And there was a lot of Russian coming from Russia to Poland, which married Polish and, and want to go to Israel. But the first Aliyah was the-- very, very many people, Russian women with husbands went to Israel. Some of them got through, some of them not. But they-- What, what happened to us is-- you see, my husband wasn't very strong. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the time we-- I got-- you see, with me, it was very bad. Because while I was in concentration camp, I didn't have period, and I was sure I never have children. When we married, of course, we went out of kibbutz. We got a flat, and we lived in flat. And I tried to get pregnant, but-- and we decided to stay for a while in Poland, not to go with the first Aliyah. And we told that we won't go on the first Aliyah. Later on, we might go to Israel. They said it's OK, it's OK. There is only first, but there will be a lot, so it's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed in Vrotslav?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed in Vrotslav

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stay in Vrotslav, yeah. In Vrotslav in 1950, I give birth of my daughter, Esther, and 1952, I give birth of my son, Mark. And we live in Vrotslav. I was-- my husband was working as a tailor in a big factory. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your husband to survivor, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband survived?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We, we went to Russia. He didn't have problems. He was survivor, because he never was in occupation area. Yes. And what I, I started-- yes, and then we decided to stay for a while in Poland and see what happen. And when I got two children-- so I was busy with raising children. My husband was working, and I was looking after the children. Of course, I have sometimes-- I was very active in-- and house where we lived was-- you see, I knew Russian, and I knew Polish by that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I start to, to work with a group of-- from Jewish theater. We went on the road with Jewish songs. I was a- a- a accordion player, and I was accompaniment for a singer. [INAUDIBLE], there was others, and we went-- I have even in paper, newspaper, my name. We went around kibbutz and all Jewish organization and give them a concert, and mostly in Jewish, you see? So that was my-- but I, I got good money. For my two concerts, I got more than my husband for a whole month in working, but that's besides the point. So we just stayed in Poland, and stayed in Poland till-- Did you experience any antisemitism in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, Oh, yes. The first thing, why I come here, it was antisemitism in Poland. Yes. And they, they couldn't say I'm Jew if I don't say. But one day, my daughter went to first grade at school and come with a cry. Mom! They told me, Jew, go to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I heard this, that was-- I have heard this. I stay in the queue, and they say-- and they said and Polish, Jew is [INAUDIBLE]. Normal. So I could feel. Sometimes I just put it away, like-- but sometimes I couldn't take it, and I just tell, what did I do to you! [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it bugs me very much. That's the reason I said-- when my daughter come home with a cry, I said, oh, no. We're not going to stay here. That's for sure. And then my husband-- my husband said he got in Australia a distant cousin, but he doesn't know. He knows only name, but he doesn't know where is he. But I got-- had a girlfriend. We had children the same. We used to go to the park with them and this. And she said, I'm going to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. When I've heard she goes to Australia, so I told her, look, my husband know somebody there, but-- he knows the name. He knows that he is in Australia. But he doesn't know where is he. Would you mind to write me when you come to Australia and-- so that we can correspond? And she said, by all means, Lisa. I will do it. So she went, and in the meantime, so we had a choice to go to Israel, but that was open door. Anybody Jewish could go to Israel. But we have already brother-- my brother-in-law is in Israel, or the brother of my husband is-- was in Israel. And he wrote me a very bad-- it was a very hard time in Israel, you know. In 1957, we went, and it was pretty hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was a very good tailor with Hebrew. He spoke Hebrew, and he couldn't get a job. And I have to-- then he's a little-- and had to do stones to take, and also and he was a bit bitter. And that's-- when he wrote the letter, my husband, we-- he will help me what he could. From-- for Poland, we send them some parcels with food. And all-- we got from America some sending us dresses, all things. All went to Israel. He wanted to help his brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we sent parcels, but my husband was thinking twice to go or not. He wasn't very strong health, you know. So I said, look, we have to find somebody in Australia. If you find-- but we don't going to stay here. I can't stay here. I hate it. And he decided that I'm right, too, because he didn't like it, either. So I, I receive a letter from her, and I had another girlfriend. She received from her. And I said, [INAUDIBLE], give me the address. Before I got from her letter, she got a letter. So I said, give me a letter. I want to write her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote a letter, and I put the name of his cousin. And when he come and all sort of things, whatever he remember. [INAUDIBLE] and this thing. And I wrote her, please, help me to find this person. Because then with no relatives, you couldn't come to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sent her a letter, and you know, after about three weeks I received from her a letter that she put in the newspaper, a Jewish newspaper, this all down. And she expect that somebody, if you wanted, will. And yes, after a month, we receive a letter from his cousin, that he lives there and there. And I walk up avenue, I remember he doesn't live anymore in Melbourne, and that why do you want to come to Australia, he ask us.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wrote him why. We have-- but he was a pro-communist, most probably, because he couldn't understand. From Poland to Australia? And when I wrote him a letter, that the first because it's very antisemitic. Second, there is no future for my children. And third, we want to go out of here. It's not place to live for Jew. So he is, OK, if you decided. But think twice. Think twice, but if you decided, I will help you. It took him three months to decide to help me. What he helped? He sent us a permit. And because he was rich, the relief took $100. He will never forgot. I brought him money back, but he wouldn't take it. But I will never forget. And I said, what do I have to take so that I have something, because I have a-- I didn't pay for a piano. Because I was a pianist, I have the best piano you can get, [INAUDIBLE], from the government. They gave me a concert piano. Beautiful piano. Here it costs a lot of money. And I wanted to take with him. So he said, well, where you will put this piano? So that's my-- that's my hello. Why, why do you come, and where you put? Of course I knew that I go on my own, and I can-- my husband says maybe. I said, Gershon, I can bet you anything, no maybe, we have to stay on our own two feet. We come with no money. We had eight shillings, which we spent straight away to take a few luggage that we brought from Poland, from this, this-- from the ship. That was that.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. I don't know if--
